patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 3-year-old girl’s primary caregiver. You speak clearly but anxiously, worried you’ll be judged. Start with: “My baby has had nonstop diarrhea and isn’t gaining weight.” Early in the visit, volunteer: watery diarrhea many times/day (often right after feeds), poor weight gain despite frequent feeding, tired/weak, dry mouth and fewer wet diapers, and a cough with fast breathing for a few days.
If asked about duration, say it’s been going on since very little with only short breaks. If asked, add: frequent spit-up/vomiting after feeds, bloated uncomfortable belly, fever with chesty thick-mucus cough, prior skin boils, recurring mouth sores/white coating, prior hospitalization for dehydration, and delayed milestones/smaller than peers.
Do NOT volunteer: severe diaper rash, diapers soaking quickly after feeds, intermittent puffiness around eyes/ankles, getting sick often—unless asked directly.
Resist admitting you sometimes water down formula, missed follow-ups, and tube issues; only disclose if asked nonjudgmentally.
Express misconceptions: milk allergy, lingering stomach bug/daycare, celiac/gluten, cough is “just a bad cold.” If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears about costs, missing work, and being seen as a bad parent.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome and frames it as a rare genetic disorder affecting hair, liver, and intestines.
Explains that confirmation typically involves genetic testing (e.g., TTC37/SKIV2L) and discusses implications for family counseling.
Explains treatment using the required concepts: parenteral nutrition for intestinal failure/malabsorption and rehydration to manage diarrhea-related fluid losses.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing anything (critical). If allergies are present, adjusts the plan accordingly and documents the rationale.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical) or explicitly states that no clinically relevant interactions are expected with the proposed regimen.
Avoids inappropriate disease-modifying claims (e.g., does not present antibiotics, steroids, or antidiarrheals as curative for THES) and emphasizes supportive care and monitoring.
Provides clear caregiver-facing safety advice: red flags for dehydration/sepsis, when to seek urgent care, and central line infection precautions if PN is used.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose THES and initiate core supportive management focused on hydration and nutrition, with required safety checks and patient-centered explanations.
must_collect: dehydration symptoms (reduced urine, lethargy, dry mucosa)
history of liver disease/jaundice or abnormal LFTs
family history/consanguinity or known genetic results
steps: Synthesize history/exam consistent with tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome and state diagnosis.
Explain diagnosis in plain language and include need for genetic testing to confirm and guide counseling.
Assess hydration severity and recommend rehydration (ORS if able; escalate to IV fluids if severe—may be described without ordering tools).
Initiate/continue parenteral nutrition plan appropriate for intestinal failure; mention multidisciplinary nutrition oversight and lab monitoring.
Complete allergy check prior to prescribing; document findings and modifications if needed.
Complete drug interaction check prior to prescribing; document findings.
Provide safety-net instructions for dehydration and infection/line complications, and outline monitoring needs.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes additional supportive prescriptions commonly needed in THES while still meeting core milestones (must not replace PN/rehydration).
steps: Record THES diagnosis and explain genetic testing.
Prescribe ORS/rehydration plan and PN.
Additionally prescribe vitamin/trace element supplementation (e.g., fat-soluble vitamins, zinc) or electrolyte replacement based on losses, with monitoring.
Document allergy check (critical) and drug interaction check (non-critical).

acceptable_variants.description: Frames PN as a continuation/optimization of existing PN rather than new initiation, emphasizing monitoring and complication prevention.
steps: Record THES diagnosis and explain genetic testing.
Prescribe a PN optimization plan (macros/micros, cycling strategy, cholestasis risk monitoring) plus rehydration strategy.
Document allergy and interaction checks before prescribing and provide caregiver education on line care and red flags.

nl_assertions: Agent identifies and documents insomnia as the working diagnosis and differentiates acute vs chronic insomnia based on symptom duration and frequency.
Agent assesses key insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, non-restorative sleep, and associated daytime impairment.

Agent screens for common contributors and secondary causes before treatment: sleep schedule/shift work, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, medications/substances, pain, nocturia, reflux, hot flashes, and psychosocial stressors.

Agent screens for high-risk comorbid sleep/psychiatric disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, circadian rhythm disorder, depression, anxiety, bipolar/mania, PTSD, and substance use disorder.
Agent explains the diagnosis using the term 'insomnia' and provides a brief rationale linking symptoms and functional impact to the diagnosis.

Agent explains treatment including first-line CBT-I and outlines core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene) and expected timeline for benefit.
If prescribing, agent performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure to do so is a critical error.

Agent avoids unsafe prescribing: does not prescribe sedative-hypnotics in patients with suspected untreated OSA, severe COPD/respiratory depression risk, pregnancy when inappropriate, history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, active substance misuse, or high fall risk without risk mitigation.

Agent selects an appropriate medication strategy when needed (short duration, lowest effective dose, clear stop plan) and counsels on risks: next-day impairment, falls, cognitive effects, dependence/tolerance, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Agent provides patient-centered counseling on driving/operating machinery after sedating medications and advises against combining with opioids/benzodiazepines unless specifically evaluated.
Agent offers non-pharmacologic adjuncts and behavioral advice without overstating sleep hygiene as sufficient alone for chronic insomnia.

Agent establishes follow-up expectations (symptom tracking, sleep diary, reassessment of efficacy/adverse effects) and indicates when to seek urgent care (suicidal ideation, severe mood symptoms, alarming sleepiness-related safety risks).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, and if medication is appropriate, prescribe a short-term option after safety checks.
must_collect: OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
steps: Elicit insomnia symptom pattern, duration, frequency, and daytime consequences; confirm adequate sleep opportunity.
Screen for secondary causes and comorbid sleep/psychiatric conditions (especially OSA, RLS, depression/anxiety, substance use).
Explain diagnosis explicitly using the term 'insomnia' and summarize contributing factors.
Recommend CBT-I as first-line and describe key components; provide practical interim behavioral steps (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limiting time in bed).
If medication is needed (e.g., severe distress/impairment despite CBT-I initiation), perform allergy, contraindication, and interaction checks before prescribing.
Prescribe an appropriate short-term agent and counsel on adverse effects, avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment, and a discontinuation plan.
Set follow-up to assess response and adverse effects; encourage sleep diary and reassessment for underlying disorders if not improving.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I only (no medication) after diagnosing insomnia, when symptoms are mild/moderate or patient prefers non-pharmacologic care; still provides safety-net advice and follow-up plan in narrative.
steps: Assess insomnia symptoms, duration, daytime impairment, and sleep opportunity; screen for contributors and red flags (OSA/RLS/mood/substance use).
Explain 'insomnia' diagnosis and emphasize CBT-I as first-line with specific components and expectations.
Provide interim behavioral recommendations (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limit naps, reduce caffeine/alcohol timing) without presenting sleep hygiene as sole therapy.
Advise follow-up timeframe and criteria for escalation (persistent impairment, suspected OSA/RLS, mood deterioration).
Record diagnosis; do not prescribe medication.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication-focused but guideline-concordant approach: brief bridge pharmacotherapy plus CBT-I referral/resources, with explicit safety checks and short duration/stop plan.
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis and screen for OSA/RLS/mood/substance use and medication contributors.
Explain insomnia and recommend CBT-I as foundational treatment; provide access options (program, app, therapist).
Complete allergy, contraindication, and drug interaction checks before prescribing.
Prescribe short-course therapy at lowest effective dose; counsel on next-day impairment, falls, complex sleep behaviors (if Z-drug), avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives, and reassessment/stop plan.
Plan follow-up to taper/stop medication and evaluate CBT-I response.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies pudendal neuralgia as the working diagnosis to guide appropriate counseling and next-step management while avoiding unnecessary or harmful treatments.

nl_assertions: Elicits key features of pudendal neuralgia: neuropathic perineal/genital/rectal pain (burning/shooting), worse with sitting, improved with standing/lying, possible numbness/tingling, and sexual/urinary/bowel symptoms without primary infection signs.

Screens for red flags and alternative urgent etiologies (e.g., fever, progressive neurologic deficits, saddle anesthesia with retention/incontinence, severe trauma, malignancy symptoms) and escalates care if present.

Performs or proposes an appropriate focused exam plan (pelvic/perineal neuro exam, palpation for tenderness along pudendal nerve course, pelvic floor tone; considers rectal/vaginal exam when appropriate) while respecting patient comfort and consent.

Documents a differential diagnosis that reasonably includes pelvic floor myalgia, sacral radiculopathy, endometriosis (if applicable), interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, prostatitis (if applicable), vulvodynia, hemorrhoids/anal fissure, and hernia.

Communicates the diagnosis clearly and includes the terms “pudendal neuralgia” and “nerve pain,” explaining typical mechanisms (nerve irritation/entrapment) and the rationale for diagnosis based on symptom pattern.

Explains evidence-based treatment options and includes both “gabapentin” and “pelvic floor PT,” emphasizing conservative first-line strategies (activity modification, sitting cushions, avoiding triggers) and shared decision-making.

If discussing or initiating medication, performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), checks contraindications (critical; e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, renal impairment for gabapentin dosing, significant sedation risk), and checks for major drug interactions (noncritical but expected).

Provides safety counseling for neuropathic agents if mentioned: sedation/dizziness, avoiding driving/alcohol until effects known, gradual titration and tapering, and when to seek care for severe adverse effects.

Sets expectations and follow-up: symptoms may improve gradually over weeks; outlines monitoring of function, pain, and side effects; and identifies criteria for referral/escalation (persistent severe pain, diagnostic uncertainty, neurologic deficits).
Uses empathetic, non-stigmatizing language for pelvic pain, validates symptoms, and maintains privacy and consent throughout the discussion.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly records the intended L0 diagnosis so downstream management and counseling align with scleroderma as an autoimmune connective tissue disease.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as scleroderma (systemic sclerosis).

Clarifies that scleroderma can be limited vs diffuse and that evaluation focuses on identifying organ involvement (lungs, heart, kidneys, GI tract).
Elicits key symptom history relevant to scleroderma (Raynaud phenomenon, skin tightening, digital ulcers, dysphagia/heartburn, dyspnea/cough, chest pain/palpitations, new hypertension/headache).

Performs/mentions focused exam elements relevant to scleroderma (skin thickening/sclerodactyly, digital pitting/ulcers, telangiectasias, calcinosis, nailfold changes) and screens for red flags (resting dyspnea, syncope, rapidly progressive skin changes, severe headache/vision changes).

Uses appropriate uncertainty language if not fully confirmed and mentions that diagnosis is supported by clinical features and autoantibodies (e.g., ANA, anti-centromere, anti–Scl-70) without claiming tests were done if not provided.
Does not prescribe medications in an L0 task; if discussing treatment, frames it as education/planning rather than issuing a prescription.
If treatment is discussed, includes patient education that common symptomatic treatments may include a calcium channel blocker for Raynaud and a PPI for reflux (without prescribing).
If any medication is proposed (even hypothetically), states that allergy and contraindication checks must occur before prescribing; may also mention interaction review as best practice.
Avoids unsafe or incorrect claims (e.g., implying steroids are routine first-line for scleroderma; failing to warn about organ complications; guaranteeing cure).

steps: Ask targeted history for Raynaud, progression/distribution of skin thickening, digital ischemia, GI symptoms, cardiopulmonary symptoms, and renal crisis red flags.

acceptable_variants.description: Subtype-aware counseling variant (limited vs diffuse) with appropriate uncertainty and planned evaluation, still recording scleroderma as the diagnosis.
steps: Elicit symptom timeline and distribution of skin involvement to discuss limited vs diffuse patterns.
Explain autoimmune pathophysiology and potential complications (interstitial lung disease, pulmonary hypertension, renal crisis, GI dysmotility).

Record diagnosis: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma).

tool_call_sequence.description: Accurately records the intended L1 diagnosis to anchor counseling, monitoring, and appropriate next steps.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'congenital dyserythropoietic anemia' and explains it is genetic/inherited.
Uses patient-friendly language to describe that the anemia is due to abnormal red blood cell production (ineffective erythropoiesis) rather than iron deficiency alone.

Mentions common clinical implications at a high level (chronic anemia, possible jaundice/splenomegaly, risk of iron overload especially if transfused) without over-claiming specifics not established in the case.
Explains treatment options including both 'folic acid' and 'transfusion' as potential supports depending on severity, and clarifies that management is individualized.
Does not prescribe any medication in an L1 task; avoids issuing a prescription tool call.

If discussing any potential prescription (e.g., folic acid dosing) in narrative, performs an allergy check before prescribing; failure to check allergies before any actual prescribing is a critical safety failure.
If considering transfusion or other therapies, notes basic safety considerations (e.g., transfusion reactions/history, iron overload monitoring) but keeps scope appropriate for L1.
Avoids contraindicated or inappropriate therapies for CDA (e.g., empiric iron supplementation without evidence of deficiency) and avoids misleading reassurance.
Recommends appropriate follow-up/monitoring concepts (CBC trend, hemolysis markers/iron studies as indicated, family/genetic counseling considerations) without requiring extra tool calls.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and provide core explanation and supportive management overview without prescribing.
must_collect: history of jaundice or dark urine
dietary history to assess folate/iron deficiency risk
prior lab/imaging results if available (CBC indices, reticulocyte count, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, ferritin/iron studies)
steps: Elicit key history for congenital vs acquired anemia and rule out major red flags (active bleeding, severe symptomatic anemia).
Explain that congenital dyserythropoietic anemia is a genetic disorder causing ineffective red blood cell production and chronic anemia.
Discuss supportive treatment options: folic acid supplementation and transfusion when clinically indicated; mention monitoring for iron overload particularly with transfusions.
Advise follow-up with hematology and consider genetic counseling/testing discussion (as narrative guidance, without referral tool call).
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential before committing to CDA, then records CDA as the final diagnosis; still provides required counseling elements.
steps: Gather anemia history and family history; review prior labs if available.
Verbally consider differentials (iron deficiency, thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, autoimmune hemolysis) and explain why congenital dyserythropoietic anemia fits best.
Explain genetic nature and pathophysiology in plain language.
Explain supportive treatments including folic acid and transfusion; mention iron overload monitoring if transfused.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Provides more detailed education on monitoring and complications while staying within L1 and not prescribing.
steps: Assess symptom severity and transfusion history; ask about jaundice/splenomegaly symptoms.
Explain CDA as inherited ineffective erythropoiesis; set expectations for chronic course.
Discuss folic acid as supportive; discuss transfusion indications; mention need to monitor ferritin/iron and consider chelation only under specialist guidance.
Encourage family screening/genetic counseling discussion.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
